q: Describe the primary transport functions of the cardiovascular system.
a: The primary function is the transport of materials to and from all parts of the body. This includes materials entering the body (oxygen, nutrients, water), materials moved from cell to cell (wastes, immune cells, antibodies, hormones, stored nutrients), and materials leaving the body (metabolic wastes, heat, carbon dioxide).

q: Describe the organization of the cardiovascular system.
a: It is a closed circulatory system consisting of a heart (the pump), blood vessels (the tubes or vasculature), and blood (the fluid containing cells and plasma).
Location: Microscopic vessels located within tissues. Function: The site where blood exchanges material with the interstitial fluid.
Blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart.
Blood vessels that return blood to the heart.
Location: The central wall of the heart. Function: Divides the heart into independent left and right halves and prevents blood from mixing.
Location: The upper chambers of the heart. Function: Receives blood returning to the heart from the blood vessels.
Location: The lower chambers of the heart. Function: Pumps blood out into the blood vessels.
Location: Connect the right ventricle to the lungs. Function: Carry low-oxygen blood to the lungs for oxygenation.
Location: Connect the lungs to the left atrium. Function: Carry well-oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the left side of the heart.
The specific route of blood vessels that go from the right ventricle of the heart, to the lungs, and back to the left atrium.
The specific route of blood vessels that carry blood from the left side of the heart to the body tissues, and then back to the right side of the heart.
Location: The large artery exiting the left ventricle. Function: Branches into smaller arteries to distribute oxygenated blood to capillary networks throughout the body's tissues.
Location: A large vein entering the right atrium. Function: Returns blood from the upper part of the body to the heart.
Location: A large vein entering the right atrium. Function: Returns blood from the lower part of the body to the heart.
Location: The membranous sac encasing the heart. Function: Protects the heart and contains a thin layer of pericardial fluid that lubricates the external surface as it beats.
Location: The thick muscular walls of the heart. Function: The cardiac muscle responsible for generating the mechanical force of contraction.
The major blood vessel exiting the right ventricle that quickly splits into the left and right pulmonary arteries.
Location: Situated between the atria and the ventricles. Function: Ensure one-way blood flow from the atria to the ventricles and prevent backward flow during ventricular contraction.

Location: Situated between the ventricles and the major arteries (aorta and pulmonary trunk). Function: Snap closed to prevent blood from flowing backward into the ventricles from the arteries after contraction.
Location: Moundlike extensions of the ventricular muscle. Function: Tether the chordae tendineae to provide stability to the AV valves; they do not actively open or close the valves.
Location: The right AV valve, separating the right atrium and the right ventricle. It consists of three flaps.
Location: The left AV valve, separating the left atrium and the left ventricle. It consists of two flaps.
Location: The semilunar valve located between the left ventricle and the aorta.
Location: The semilunar valve located between the right ventricle and the pulmonary trunk.
The heart's dedicated blood supply, essential because of the heart's high energy and oxygen demands (extracts 70-80% of delivered oxygen at rest).

q: Define: Coronary Arteries (Right, Left, Circumflex, Anterior Interventricular)
a: Location: Branches originating from the aorta. Function: Arteries that nourish the heart muscle itself by delivering highly oxygenated blood.
Location: Running alongside coronary arteries on the surface of the heart. Function: Collect deoxygenated blood from the myocardial capillaries.
Location: On the posterior aspect of the heart. Function: A large vein that drains about 85% of the deoxygenated venous blood from the myocardium directly into the right atrium.

q: Describe the two types of myocardial cells and their arrangement in the heart.
a: 1. Contractile cells: Striated muscle organized into sarcomeres that make up the bulk of heart muscle. 2. Autorhythmic cells: Specialized cells (1%) that generate action potentials spontaneously without innervation.
Striated muscle cells organized into sarcomeres that generate the mechanical force of the heart.
Specialized, distinct cells (1% of myocardium) that generate action potentials spontaneously; signal for contraction is myogenic.

q: Explain the key role of pacemaker cells.
a: They initiate action potentials without innervation, setting the heart rate and coordinating contractions.

q: Describe the process of EC-coupling (Excitation) within cardiac muscle.
a: AP enters via gap junctions, moves into T-tubules, and opens L-type Ca2+ channels, allowing extracellular Ca2+ entry.

q: Describe the process of EC-coupling (Contraction) within cardiac muscle.
a: Ca2+ entry triggers RyR channels to release stored Ca2+ (CICR). Ca2+ binds to troponin, initiating crossbridge formation and sliding filament contraction.
Process where calcium entry through L-type channels triggers RyR channels in the sarcoplasmic reticulum to release more Ca2+.

q: Describe the process of relaxation in cardiac muscle.
a: Cytoplasmic Ca2+ decreases as Ca2+ is moved back into SR (Ca2+-ATPase) and out of cell (NCX antiporter). Ca2+ unbinds from troponin, and filaments slide back.
Ryanodine receptor channels in the sarcoplasmic reticulum that release Ca2+ into the cytosol during CICR.
Membrane protein that removes 1 Ca2+ from the cell in exchange for 3 Na+ moving in.

q: Define: Sodium-potassium pump (Na+-K+-ATPase) in cardiac EC coupling
a: Pumps Na+ out of the cell to maintain gradients used by the NCX antiporter.

q: Describe Phase 4 (Resting potential) of a contractile action potential.
a: Stable resting membrane potential at about -90 mV.

q: Describe Phase 0 (Depolarization) of a contractile action potential.
a: Rapid Na+ entry via voltage-gated channels brings potential to +20 mV.

q: Describe Phase 1 (Initial repolarization) of a contractile action potential.
a: Na+ channels close and fast K+ channels open, allowing K+ to leave.

q: Describe Phase 2 (The plateau) of a contractile action potential.
a: Ca2+ entry and decreased K+ efflux maintain a long plateau (200+ msec), preventing tetanus.

q: Describe Phase 3 (Rapid repolarization) of a contractile action potential.
a: Ca2+ channels close and slow K+ channels open, returning the cell to -90 mV.

q: Explain refractory periods and summation in cardiac muscle.
a: Long plateau creates a long refractory period lasting almost as long as the muscle twitch, preventing summation and tetanus.
Unstable membrane potential (-60 mV) that drifts toward threshold in autorhythmic cells.

q: Describe the mechanism of the Pacemaker Potential.
a: If channels open at -60 mV, allowing Na+ influx to exceed K+ efflux, causing slow depolarization.

q: Describe AP firing in autorhythmic cells.
a: Threshold triggers voltage-gated Ca2+ channels to open (steep depolarization); repolarization is via slow K+ channels.
HCN channels permeable to Na+ and K+ that open at -60 mV to initiate pacemaker potential.

q: Compare autorhythmic vs. contractile action potentials.
a: Contractile: Stable RMP, Na+ depolarization, plateau phase. Autorhythmic: Unstable potential, Ca2+ depolarization, no plateau.

q: Describe electrical conduction through the heart.
a: SA node -> Internodal pathway -> AV node -> AV bundle -> Bundle branches -> Purkinje fibers.
The main pacemaker in the right atrium that sets the heart rate.
Pathway connecting SA node to AV node, spreading signals across atria.
Gateway to ventricles; slows signal (AV delay) to allow atrial completion.
Slowing of the AP at the AV node to ensure atria finish contracting before ventricles begin.
Transmits signals from AV node down the ventricular septum.
Left and right branches from the AV bundle that lead toward the apex.
Rapid conductors from apex to base, ensuring near-simultaneous apex contraction.
Time during which cardiac muscle relaxes.
Time during which cardiac muscle contracts.
Atria and ventricles relaxed; AV valves open; blood flows passively into ventricles.
Atria contract (P wave), forcing final 20% of blood into ventricles.
End-diastolic volume; max blood in ventricles (~135 mL).
Ventricles contract (QRS); AV valves close (S1); semilunar valves closed; volume constant.
S1 (lub) caused by closure of AV valves.
Ventricular pressure > arterial pressure; semilunar valves open; blood ejected.
End-systolic volume; min blood in ventricles (~65 mL).
Ventricles relax (T wave); semilunar valves close (S2); volume constant until AV open.
S2 (dup) caused by closure of semilunar valves.
Summed electrical activity including P wave, QRS complex, and T wave.
Represents atrial depolarization.
Represents ventricular depolarization; hides atrial repolarization.
Represents ventricular repolarization.
Represents AV node delay; atrial contraction occurs here.
Segment between ventricular depolarization and repolarization; site of ischemia changes.
Contraction raises ventricular pressure above arterial, opening valves for flow.
Stroke Volume = EDV - ESV.
Ejection Fraction = SV / EDV.
Cardiac Output = Heart Rate x Stroke Volume.
ACh binds muscarinic receptors; increases K+ efflux/decreases Ca2+ influx; slows HR.
NE binds beta-1 receptors; cAMP keeps If channels open longer; increases Na+/Ca2+ influx; speeds HR.
Phosphorylation enhances SR Ca2+-ATPase, causing stronger and shorter contractions.
